case_name: AZUGA INC & ANR. .....Plaintiffs versus AZUGA TELEMATICS LLC & ORS. .....Defendants
court: High Court of Delhi at New Delhi
facts: Plaintiffs Azuga Inc. (California) and Azuga Telematics Private Limited (Bangalore) sued Azuga Telematics LLC (Jamshedpur) and others for trademark infringement, passing off and copyright infringement alleging use of the registered mark 'AZUGA', copying of website UI and images, and use of the trade name 'AZUGA TELEMATICS'. Plaintiffs say Defendant No.1 used identical services and the plaintiffs' images and trademarks on its website and job listings, and that Defendant No.3 is an ex-employee connected to Defendant No.1. Plaintiffs pursued pre-litigation notices and a mediation petition which failed, and sought interim relief including domain suspension and an injunction against further use of the mark and copied content.
arguments_claimant: Plaintiffs contended they are the registered proprietor of the mark 'AZUGA' (registration No. 6422638) with substantial goodwill and have exclusive copyright in website images; they alleged Defendants adopted the same trade name and copied UI assets and photographs, acting mala fide and causing likelihood of public confusion and reputational harm. They sought ex-parte ad-interim injunctions, domain suspension, discovery of defendants' documents and other ancillary reliefs.
arguments_respondent: Defendant No.2 (through video-conference) denied connection with Defendant No.1, stated he was an ex-employee of Plaintiff No.2 and that registrant details on the domain page did not pertain to him; registration details were noted as expired. Defendant responses generally included factual denials and limited admission statements pending filings; Defendant No.4 (domain registrar) was directed to provide registrant/Billing/BSI details and to lock the domain.
ratio_decidendi: The Court's legal question was whether ad-interim injunction should be granted against the Defendants for trademark and copyright infringement. The Court applied the settled injunction test, holding that "The Plaintiffs have made out a prima facie case for the grant of ad-interim injunction, balance of convenience lies in favour of the Plaintiffs and irreparable harm will be caused to the Plaintiffs, in case the restraint is not granted." The Court found on these facts that the Plaintiffs are registered proprietors and that Defendants' adoption of the name 'AZUGA' and copying of images "prima facie appears mala fide" and is likely to cause confusion. The injunction was therefore granted on an interim basis, subject to further hearing and specified compliance directions.
final_order: The Court issued an ad-interim injunction restraining Defendant Nos.1 and 3 (and their associates) from using marks identical or deceptively similar to 'AZUGA' and from copying Plaintiffs' copyrighted images, directed Defendant No.4 (domain registrar) to lock and suspend https://azugatelematicsllc.com/, issued notices to Defendants 1,3 and 4, ordered discovery and filings on fixed timetables and listed the matter for further dates (24.11.2025 before Joint Registrar and 30.03.2026 before the Court).
summary: This is a 2025 Delhi High Court commercial suit where Azuga Inc. and Azuga Telematics Pvt Ltd sued a Jamshedpur company and others for trademark and copyright infringement and passing off. The court found a prima facie case, balance of convenience and likely irreparable harm and granted an ad-interim injunction restraining the defendants from using the 'AZUGA' mark and from copying Plaintiffs' website images, and ordered the domain to be locked. The order is interim, with discovery directed and further dates scheduled.
embedding_summary: Azuga Inc & Anr v Azuga Telematics LLC & Ors, High Court of Delhi, 26 September 2025, CS(COMM) 1044/2025 & I.A. 24393-24399/2025. Plaintiffs (Azuga Inc, Azuga Telematics Pvt Ltd) alleged trademark infringement of registered mark 'AZUGA' (Regn. No. 6422638), passing off and copyright infringement (Sections 2(c) and 13(1)(a) Copyright Act, 1957; Section 29(5) Trade Marks Act, 1999) by Defendant No.1's website https://azugatelematicsllc.com which copied UI assets and images and used the trade name 'AZUGA TELEMATICS'. Court applied Order XXXIX Rules 1&2 CPC injunction principles and held "The Plaintiffs have made out a prima facie case for the grant of ad-interim injunction, balance of convenience lies in favour of the Plaintiffs and irreparable harm will be caused to the Plaintiffs"; ordered domain lock by registrar (Defendant No.4), discovery under Order XI, and other compliance (Section 151 CPC; Order XI Rules; Section 63(4)(c) BSA compliance). Outcome: interim injunction granted, domain suspended, discovery and timelines set; no monetary compensation awarded in this order.
legal_principles: Prima facie case, balance of convenience and irreparable harm justify ad-interim injunction in trademark/copyright suits
Mala fide adoption of identical trade name and copying of copyrighted website assets supports interim injunctive relief
Court can direct domain registrars to suspend/lock a domain on prima facie infringement findings
Discovery and production under Order XI CPC and compliance with affidavit of admission/denial are essential pre-trial steps
Unjustified denial of documents may attract costs and adverse procedural consequences
sections_cited: Order XI Rule 1(4) Code of Civil Procedure, 1908
Order XI Rules 1, 3 and 5 Code of Civil Procedure, 1908
Order XXXIX Rules 1 and 2 Code of Civil Procedure, 1908
Order XXXIX Rule 3 Code of Civil Procedure, 1908
Commercial Courts Act, 2015 (as amendment to CPC provisions)
bm25_text: AZUGA INC & ANR. .....Plaintiffs versus AZUGA TELEMATICS LLC & ORS. .....Defendants High Court of Delhi at New Delhi 2025 Plaintiffs Azuga Inc. (California) and Azuga Telematics Private Limited (Bangalore) sued Azuga Telematics LLC (Jamshedpur) and others for trademark infringement, passing off and copyright infringement alleging use of the registered mark 'AZUGA', copying of website UI and images, and use of the trade name 'AZUGA TELEMATICS'. Plaintiffs say Defendant No.1 used identical services and the plaintiffs' images and trademarks on its website and job listings, and that Defendant No.3 is an ex-employee connected to Defendant No.1. Plaintiffs pursued pre-litigation notices and a mediation petition which failed, and sought interim relief including domain suspension and an injunction against further use of the mark and copied content. Plaintiffs contended they are the registered proprietor of the mark 'AZUGA' (registration No. 6422638) with substantial goodwill and have exclusive copyright in website images; they alleged Defendants adopted the same trade name and copied UI assets and photographs, acting mala fide and causing likelihood of public confusion and reputational harm. They sought ex-parte ad-interim injunctions, domain suspension, discovery of defendants' documents and other ancillary reliefs. Defendant No.2 (through video-conference) denied connection with Defendant No.1, stated he was an ex-employee of Plaintiff No.2 and that registrant details on the domain page did not pertain to him; registration details were noted as expired. Defendant responses generally included factual denials and limited admission statements pending filings; Defendant No.4 (domain registrar) was directed to provide registrant/Billing/BSI details and to lock the domain. The Court's legal question was whether ad-interim injunction should be granted against the Defendants for trademark and copyright infringement. The Court applied the settled injunction test, holding that "The Plaintiffs have made out a prima facie case for the grant of ad-interim injunction, balance of convenience lies in favour of the Plaintiffs and irreparable harm will be caused to the Plaintiffs, in case the restraint is not granted." The Court found on these facts that the Plaintiffs are registered proprietors and that Defendants' adoption of the name 'AZUGA' and copying of images "prima facie appears mala fide" and is likely to cause confusion. The injunction was therefore granted on an interim basis, subject to further hearing and specified compliance directions. The Court issued an ad-interim injunction restraining Defendant Nos.1 and 3 (and their associates) from using marks identical or deceptively similar to 'AZUGA' and from copying Plaintiffs' copyrighted images, directed Defendant No.4 (domain registrar) to lock and suspend https://azugatelematicsllc.com/, issued notices to Defendants 1,3 and 4, ordered discovery and filings on fixed timetables and listed the matter for further dates (24.11.2025 before Joint Registrar and 30.03.2026 before the Court). This is a 2025 Delhi High Court commercial suit where Azuga Inc. and Azuga Telematics Pvt Ltd sued a Jamshedpur company and others for trademark and copyright infringement and passing off. The court found a prima facie case, balance of convenience and likely irreparable harm and granted an ad-interim injunction restraining the defendants from using the 'AZUGA' mark and from copying Plaintiffs' website images, and ordered the domain to be locked. The order is interim, with discovery directed and further dates scheduled. Azuga Inc & Anr v Azuga Telematics LLC & Ors, High Court of Delhi, 26 September 2025, CS(COMM) 1044/2025 & I.A. 24393-24399/2025. Plaintiffs (Azuga Inc, Azuga Telematics Pvt Ltd) alleged trademark infringement of registered mark 'AZUGA' (Regn. No. 6422638), passing off and copyright infringement (Sections 2(c) and 13(1)(a) Copyright Act, 1957; Section 29(5) Trade Marks Act, 1999) by Defendant No.1's website https://azugatelematicsllc.com which copied UI assets and images and used the trade name 'AZUGA TELEMATICS'. Court applied Order XXXIX Rules 1&2 CPC injunction principles and held "The Plaintiffs have made out a prima facie case for the grant of ad-interim injunction, balance of convenience lies in favour of the Plaintiffs and irreparable harm will be caused to the Plaintiffs"; ordered domain lock by registrar (Defendant No.4), discovery under Order XI, and other compliance (Section 151 CPC; Order XI Rules; Section 63(4)(c) BSA compliance). Outcome: interim injunction granted, domain suspended, discovery and timelines set; no monetary compensation awarded in this order. Prima facie case, balance of convenience and irreparable harm justify ad-interim injunction in trademark/copyright suits Mala fide adoption of identical trade name and copying of copyrighted website assets supports interim injunctive relief Court can direct domain registrars to suspend/lock a domain on prima facie infringement findings Discovery and production under Order XI CPC and compliance with affidavit of admission/denial are essential pre-trial steps Unjustified denial of documents may attract costs and adverse procedural consequences Order XI Rule 1(4) Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 Order XI Rules 1, 3 and 5 Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 Order XXXIX Rules 1 and 2 Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 Order XXXIX Rule 3 Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 Section 151 Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 Section 29(5) Trade Marks Act, 1999 Section 2(c) Copyright Act, 1957 Section 13(1)(a) Copyright Act, 1957 Section 63(4)(c) Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023 Commercial Courts Act, 2015 (as amendment to CPC provisions) Delhi High Court (Original Side) Rules, 2018
